coercive.definition: Forcing you to respond; impossible to ignore.
coercive.example: A baby's cry is coercive because you cannot help but respond.

demand.definition: A strong request that expects to be obeyed.
demand.example: The baby's cry sounds like a demand for attention.

biologically.definition: In a way related to the natural processes of living things.
biologically.example: We are biologically programmed to respond to a baby's cry.

programmed.definition: Set up to automatically do something in a certain way.
programmed.example: Humans are programmed by nature to care for their babies.

species.definition: A group of living things that are the same kind.
species.example: Responding to babies is essential to the survival of the species.

alarm.definition: A warning signal that something is wrong.
alarm.example: The baby's cry is like an alarm telling you they need help.

zebra.definition: A black-and-white striped animal from Africa, similar to a horse.
zebra.example: A zebra in the herd warns the others when danger is near.

herd.definition: A large group of animals that live and move together.
herd.example: The zebra communicated danger to the rest of the herd.

subtle.example: A baby's emotions are not subtle; they cry loudly.

desperate.definition: Feeling that you have no hope and need help urgently.
desperate.example: The baby sounds desperate because they truly are.

jeopardizing.definition: Putting something in danger of being harmed or lost.
jeopardizing.example: Ignoring a baby's cry is jeopardizing their development.

intimate.example: Intimate companionship is vital to a baby's growth.

companionship.definition: The state of having someone with you; being together.
companionship.example: Babies need intimate companionship to develop properly.

vital.example: Responding to your baby is vital for their brain development.

surrendering.definition: Giving in; allowing something to happen without fighting it.
surrendering.example: Surrendering to the process of caring for a baby can bring up old feelings.

spectrum.definition: A range between two extreme points.
spectrum.example: You may feel anything along the spectrum from unsettled to despairing.

unsettled.example: New parents often feel unsettled in the first weeks.

despairing.definition: Feeling that there is no hope.
despairing.example: She felt despairing when the baby would not stop crying.

contain.definition: To hold feelings safely without being overwhelmed by them.
contain.example: You need someone to contain your feelings so you can contain your baby's.

dissociate.definition: To disconnect your mind from your feelings or experience as a way of coping.
dissociate.example: Babies who are left alone may dissociate from their distress.

cortisol.definition: A stress hormone made by the body when you feel afraid or under pressure.
cortisol.example: Even after the baby stops crying, their cortisol levels remain high.

mammalian.definition: Related to mammals, the group of animals that feed their young with milk.
mammalian.example: Dissociating from distress is a mammalian survival mechanism.

survival.example: Crying is a survival mechanism for babies.

mechanism.definition: A natural process or system that does a particular thing.
mechanism.example: Dissociation is a survival mechanism in response to extreme stress.

flashbacks.definition: Sudden, unwanted memories of a past experience that feel very real.
flashbacks.chinese: 閃回記憶；創傷回憶重現
flashbacks.example: People who dissociate from distress can experience flashbacks later.

haunt.definition: To keep coming back to your mind in a troubling way.
haunt.example: Unprocessed feelings can come back to haunt you.

trigger.definition: Something that causes a strong emotional reaction, often connected to the past.
trigger.example: Having a baby can trigger feelings from your own childhood.

disconcerting.definition: Making you feel confused, worried, or uncomfortable.
disconcerting.example: The sudden rush of emotions was disconcerting.

triggered.definition: Caused to happen; set off an emotional reaction.
triggered.example: Old feelings can be triggered by caring for your own baby.

adulthood.definition: The time of life when you are a grown-up.
adulthood.example: Feelings from childhood may surface later in adulthood.

angering.example: Reading this may be angering you if you feel guilty.

frightening.example: Learning about the effects of crying it out can be frightening.

repair.definition: To fix or make better after damage.
repair.example: You can repair the situation by taking your child's feelings seriously.

dismiss.definition: To treat something as unimportant; to ignore.
dismiss.example: Do not dismiss your child's feelings as silly.

defensive.definition: Protecting yourself from criticism; not willing to admit mistakes.
defensive.example: If the parent is not defensive, the healing is more powerful.

blaming.definition: Saying someone else is responsible for something bad.
blaming.example: A parent who is not blaming creates a safe space for healing.

medicine.definition: Here, something that heals or makes things better (used as a metaphor).
medicine.example: Being taken seriously by your parent is powerful medicine.

synchronize.definition: To make things happen or work together at the same time.
synchronize.example: We can never synchronize to our baby as perfectly as the womb.

inevitably.definition: In a way that cannot be avoided; certain to happen.
inevitably.example: There will inevitably be misunderstandings between parent and baby.

ruptures.definition: Breaks or disruptions in a relationship or bond.
ruptures.example: It is not the ruptures that matter most but how they are repaired.

facilitate.definition: To make something easier or help it happen.
facilitate.example: We try to facilitate the development of our child's sense of security.

transition.definition: A change from one state or condition to another.
transition.example: The transition from the womb to the outside world is huge for a baby.

utero.definition: Inside the mother's womb (from Latin 'in utero').
utero.example: Life in utero is perfectly tuned to the baby's needs.

loneliness.definition: The sad feeling of being alone or not having someone close.
loneliness.example: Loneliness is a feeling that needs to be attended to, just like hunger.

discomfort.definition: A mild feeling of pain or unease.
discomfort.example: Loneliness is a feeling like discomfort that tells you something is wrong.

attending.definition: Giving care and attention to something.
attending.example: Loneliness needs attending to for a person to stay healthy.
